SWORD (Southwest Ohio Regional Depository) is a closed-stacks storage depository, not a public-access library -- it has no public walk-in hours and can't be browsed in person [1]. Materials stored there are requested through interlibrary loan and delivered to your campus library for pickup [2]. For reference, SWORD is located at 4200 N. Search course reserves in Primo: type the textbook title, a course abbreviation (e.g. ECO 201), or your professor's last name [1]. Loan periods for reserve items are chosen by the instructor -- 2-hour checkout for use in the library, 1-day, or 3-day -- and all reserve materials are removed at the end of every semester [1]. The reserves guide has the full details, including how instructors place materials on reserve [1].

A reserve textbook loan period is **2 hours, 1 day, or 3 days**, depending on the instructor [1].

I can't run catalog searches for you, but Primo -- the library catalog -- can do it in one box: https://ohiolink-mu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/search?vid=01OHIOLINK_MU:MU. It covers our books, ebooks, articles, media, and OhioLINK partner libraries. A librarian on Ask Us can also help you find materials.

To find a specific book, article, journal, or DVD at Miami University Libraries, search Primo -- the library catalog. It searches across our physical collection, ebooks, journal articles, and OhioLINK partner libraries in one place.

Primo: https://ohiolink-mu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/search?vid=01OHIOLINK_MU:MU

If Primo says "no results" and you think the library should have it, you can request it through Interlibrary Loan: https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/use/borrow/ill/

To find articles or browse research databases, use the library's Databases A-Z list. It's the authoritative index of every database the library subscribes to -- organized alphabetically and by subject -- and links directly into each database with proper authentication.

Why send you there instead of looking up a specific database name? Database names overlap and shift ("Web of Science" vs "Web of Knowledge", "PsycINFO" vs "Psychology and Behavioral Sciences Collection"); the A-Z page disambiguates them with current names + subject tags.

Databases A-Z: https://libguides.lib.miamioh.edu/az/databases

King Library's quiet/silent floor is on the THIRD FLOOR [1].

Yes -- King Library has lockers in the Reading Rooms. They are restricted to active faculty and actively enrolled graduate students. Locker assignments are requested via an online form on the Reading Rooms page and are assigned yearly on a first-come, first-served basis (with a waitlist when full) [1].

Miami University Libraries' building policies -- food and drink, alcohol, sleeping/napping, noise, pets and service animals, smoking/vaping, bikes and skateboards, and more -- are in the Libraries' Facilities & Events Policies guide [1].
Use the Printing & WiFi page for the current laptop printing instructions [1]. It is the canonical Oxford printing page [1].
Use the Printing & WiFi page for the current WiFi information [1].
Yes, color printing is available. Use the Printing & WiFi page for the current Oxford printing details [1].
You can scan a document at King Library in Oxford; the King Library directory lists scanners among its equipment [1]. The Printing & WiFi page is the current technology page for Oxford libraries [1].

Yes — at Oxford, the libraries offer technology you can check out, and Chromebook laptops may be checked out for 30 days [1]. The libraries say to come to a checkout desk with your university ID to borrow equipment [1].
Chromebook laptops may be checked out for 30 days [1].

Yes — the Oxford libraries offer chargers and cables for phones/laptops to check out, along with other technology equipment [1][2][1]. You can borrow them at a library checkout desk with your university ID [1].

The software available on library computers is listed on the Library software page [1]. It includes Windows and Mac software, and the page also notes that Adobe Creative Cloud access requires a license checkout [1].
Yes. The software page says additional titles such as MATLAB can be added to library computers using Software Center on campus Windows PCs and Self-Service on campus Macs [1].
Yes — SPSS can be added to library computers yourself. On campus Windows PCs, use Software Center; on campus Macs, use Self-Service [1].
Use the APA citation resources page for website citations [1]. It is the library’s APA guide page, and it’s the place to follow for the current format guidance [1].
Use the citation resources page for APA help; it points to recommended generators/tools [1][2]. The APA guide and the main citation LibGuide are both official Miami Libraries pages [1][2].
Yes — the citation resources page includes Zotero/EndNote tutorials [1]. You can also use the library’s citation resources page for related help [2].
Yes -- librarians offer research consultations. Find the subject librarian for your course, major, or topic on the subject librarians page and contact them directly to set up an appointment [1].

You can get research help through the Libraries’ “Ask a Librarian” service via live online assistance, email, phone, or in person at an MU Library (service hours vary—check current times on the page) [1]. You can also schedule help by finding your subject librarian on the Liaisons page [2].

You can get research help through the Libraries’ Ask a Librarian (Ask Us) service, which offers live online assistance, email help, and phone support options [1].

For Oxford, you can call King Library at (513) 529-4141 or Wertz Art/Architecture Library at (513) 529-6638 [1].

Yes — Miami University Libraries (Oxford) offers Data Services, including consultations for data analysis and support for GIS and data visualization [1]. You can also use the shortcut https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/GetMeData/ to get to the Data Services page [2].

Yes — Oxford Library data services offers consultations, including GIS help and data visualization support [1]. Library staff are also happy to help with GIS projects, and the GIS resources article says to contact Eric Johnson or the Center for Digital Scholarship for more information [2].
Oxford Library data services offers consultations and support for data analysis [1]. The page also notes help with GIS and data visualization [1].

Yes — Miami University librarians provide in-class instruction, workshops, and individual consultations in information literacy and research skills [1]. To schedule a session, use the Library Instruction Request Form on the instruction page [1].

To schedule a library instruction session, fill out the Library Instruction Request Form [1]. The page also says you can contact the Subject Specialist Librarian in your area of study if you prefer [1].
For the Dean of University Libraries and the library administration/leadership team, see the Dean's Office page [1].
The Libraries' staff directory is on the staff page -- you can look up any staff member and their contact information there [1].
Alea Henle is the Head, Access & Borrow Department, which encompasses circulation [1][2]. The Oxford staff directory is also the place to look up current circulation leadership [3].
No. King Library is **Closed** on Saturday (2026-07-18) [1].

Library hours vary by building and by term, so none of the libraries are routinely open 24 hours. King Library is the only building that runs near-24-hour schedules, and only during finals periods. Please check the hours page for the building and date you need [1].

Yes—you can place a recall/hold request through your Miami University Libraries account in OhioLINK/Primo [1]. Log in at https://ohiolink-mu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/account?vid=01OHIOLINK_MU:MU&section=overview&lang=en to submit the request [1]. For recall details and policies, see the circulation policies/recall information at https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/policies/recalling and https://libguides.lib.miamioh.edu/mul-circulation-policies [2][3].

For Miami University Libraries (Oxford), the lost-item fees and replacement procedure are covered on the Circulation Policies pages (see the main Circulation Policies guide and its related pages) [1][2]. For help with a specific lost item, contact the circulation desk at the library you borrowed it from [1][2].

Miami University Libraries does not issue an alumni library card. For the borrowing options currently available after graduation, please check the circulation policies page [1], or ask the circulation desk at (513) 529-4141.

The circulation policies page says held books are typically kept on the hold shelf for 7 days after they’re ready for pickup [1]. If you need the exact policy wording or help with a specific item, the same page is the right place to check [1].
You can return books after hours at the exterior drop boxes at the main entrances of King Library in Oxford [1][2]. King Library is the Oxford location at 151 S. Campus Ave [1].
Yes -- scanners is available at all three campuses: Oxford (King), Hamilton (Rentschler), and Middletown (Gardner-Harvey). [1][2][3]
Yes. Hamilton’s library services pages include course reserves [1][2]. For more details, see the course reserves & textbooks guide [3].

On the Oxford campus, King Library has a designated quiet/silent floor on the THIRD FLOOR [1]. You can also use the library’s reading rooms as quiet study spaces [2].
Yes — Wertz Art & Architecture Library offers printing [1]. For current printing details, use the Printing & WiFi page [2].

You can scan documents using the library’s multifunction printers/scanners (scan-to-email is part of the library scanning workflow) [1]. For the current scan-to-email steps and any requirements, use the Printing & WiFi page: https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/use/technology/printing/ [1].

King Library’s location page says it has “Computer labs and printing/scanning” and lists services and technology at King [1]. For microfilm viewing, use King’s location page for the current access details and request process [1].
I don't have a reliable answer to that. You can ask a librarian directly through Ask Us.

Yes—headphones are available for checkout at Miami University Libraries (Oxford). You can borrow equipment by going to a library checkout desk with your university ID; calling ahead to check availability is recommended (513) 529-4141 [1]. Headphones are listed as available for checkout on the Libraries’ equipment list [2].

Access to King Library after midnight depends on the posted hours for that date (it is not guaranteed just by having a Miami ID) [1][2]. Today (Thursday, 2026-07-16), King Library’s posted hours are **7:30am to 9:00pm**, so the building is not open after midnight tonight [2]. For dates with extended hours (e.g., during finals), check the current schedule on the Library Hours page [2].

The King Library location page is the right place to check for a nursing or lactation space [1]. King Library is the Oxford library, and its current location page lists services and contact details [1].
I don't have a reliable answer to that. You can ask a librarian directly through Ask Us.
Yes — the King Library location page is the place to check for floor descriptions and any floor-plan information [1]. King Library is at 151 S. Campus Ave, Oxford, OH 45056 [1].

Yes. King Library’s location page says study seating has outlets widely available, and King also offers technology and equipment checkout, including borrowable chargers via the tech checkout page [1][2].

Miami University Libraries' building policies -- food and drink, alcohol, sleeping/napping, noise, pets and service animals, smoking/vaping, bikes and skateboards, and more -- are in the Libraries' Facilities & Events Policies guide [1].
Use Miami University Libraries’ citation guide for MLA help [1]. It covers MLA among other styles, so you can use that guide for the current website-citation instructions [1].

For Chicago Manual of Style help, use the Libraries’ Citation Resources LibGuide: https://libguides.lib.miamioh.edu/citation [1]. If you’d like one-on-one help, you can contact a librarian through the Ask Us options (chat/email/appointment/phone) here: https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/research/research-support/ask/ [2].

Most article databases (EBSCO, JSTOR, and others) have a 'peer-reviewed' or 'scholarly journals' checkbox filter -- apply it to limit your results to peer-reviewed articles. Pick a database from the Databases A-Z list [1], and if you're not sure which database fits your topic, your subject librarian can recommend one [2].

Miami master's theses are in Miami University Libraries’ digital collections, which includes a “Miami University Theses & Dissertation” collection [1]. The operator note also points you to Miami’s institutional repository (Scholarly Commons) and the OhioLINK ETD Center for theses and dissertations [1].

Yes—Miami University Libraries has government documents, and you can use the Libraries’ subject liaison directory to connect with the appropriate librarian for help finding and using government information [1].

The Tech Equipment for Checkout page says the libraries offer “Chargers & cables for phones/laptops” and other equipment that you can borrow with your Miami ID at a library checkout desk, and it recommends calling ahead to check availability at (513) 529-4141 [1]. For the most current, specific equipment list (including whether HDMI cables are available), check the Tech Equipment for Checkout page [1].

The tech checkout page lists equipment available for checkout, including laptops & tablets, audio equipment, and other items, but it does not list graphing calculators in the excerpt provided [1]. For the current equipment list, use the tech-checkout page [1].
A DSLR camera is a camera, and the tech checkout page says cameras are 24-hour checkouts [1].

The Oxford libraries’ tech checkout page lists many items you can borrow, but USB drives are not listed there [1]. If you need one, the page points you to call or stop by to check current availability for equipment at (513) 529-4141 [1].

Yes — the Oxford libraries’ tech checkout includes chargers/cables for phones, and the checkout page also lists chargers for Mac/PC/phones [1]. For current availability and the full equipment list, see the Tech Equipment for Checkout page [1].

I can’t file a lost-and-found report for you, but for a lost item at King Library the place to report it is the King circulation/service desk [1]. If you’d like, you can also use Ask Us chat to connect with a librarian [2].

I can't place materials on course reserves for you -- instructors submit reserves requests themselves through the Libraries' reserves process. The Reserves & Textbooks guide has the instructor instructions for placing materials on reserve [1]. If you need help with a request, contact the circulation desk at (513) 529-4141.

I can't submit ILL requests for you -- requests go through the official form so they're tied to your account. Submit yours at https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/use/borrow/ill/ (takes a couple of minutes). Pickup is at your campus library.

3D printing at the King Library MakerSpace is often free of charge, but rates can change -- please check the current pricing on the MakerSpace guide before you print [1].

Adobe Creative Cloud access is described on the software page for student and faculty/staff use, but the provided sources do not explicitly state eligibility for part-time staff [1][2]. Please use the Adobe Creative Cloud/software page for the current eligibility details and checkout instructions [1][2].

In Miami University Libraries’ Special Collections & University Archives (Oxford), materials are used under supervised reading-room conditions, and you must follow the reading-room handling rules provided by Special Collections staff [1]. Those rules include using pencils only (no pens) and not bringing food or drink into the reading room while working with collection materials [1]. For the full reading-room guidelines and how to schedule access, see the Special Collections site [1].
Miami provides New York Times access for affiliated users. The Libraries' New York Times guide has the current activation steps — see [1].

Miami's digital exhibits and collections are listed on the Digital Collections site -- I can't reliably confirm coverage of a specific topic from here, so please browse the collections (and the past digital exhibit archive linked there) to see what's available [1].
